text: hi in today's video we discuss mean
reversion trading the concept of mean
reversion relates to the belief that
asset prices tend to return to their
long-term mean or average in trading
this suggests that a Trader can profit
by initiating trades when asset prices
are at extremes and later exit when the
prices approach mean
conversely one can also enter the mean
and exit at extremes depending on your
preferred approach
a simple example of this could be
trading a trend line let's say a Stock's
price is trending upwards and taking
support at an inclined trend line one
can buy when the price approaches the
trend line and sell when the price moves
too far away from the trend line
while trend lines can be simple to trade
trading means reversion is a much more
nuanced way of trading the markets one
that requires a lot of observation and
practice to master
let's understand where to begin when
you're trading mean reversions in stocks
the easiest way to trade mean reversions
is to trade range-bound prices if price
is gyrate between two extremes for a
prolonged period mean reversion could be
a solid money making strategy
however there are only a few stocks that
spend most of their time moving between
two price levels most of the time such
ranges have a limited life and stocks
either break out or break down therefore
it can be difficult to trade mean
reversion in range-bound prices on a
sustainable basis
trading means reversion means you're
looking for turning points in the price
you're looking for Junctions where the
price move is at an extreme is getting
exhausted and may be ripe for a reversal
to the mean or away from the mean
essentially the starting point for
trading such a strategy is to First
Define the mean
defining the mean is also the most
subjective part of the strategy because
the mean is different for different
Traders
for some it's a moving average and for
some it's the two extremes of an
oscillating indicator and for some it's
the trend line
Traders choose different trading
mechanisms to trade different mean
reversions not forgetting the various
time frames some may want to use a two
minute chart whilst others may want to
use a weekly chart
let's start with trading mean reversions
using a moving average on a weekly chart
when trading mean reversions using a
moving average Traders look for Price
moving to either extreme of a moving
average
in the simplest form they buy when the
price goes below a certain extreme and
sell when it reaches a certain extreme
above the moving average
there are however some technicalities
that the trader should consider while
trading these mean reversions
price reacts differently when it is
trending compared to when it is
consolidating in an uptrend prices will
travel more distance above the moving
average and will often take support at
the moving average the opposite will
happen in a downtrend
the moving average will itself be
upwards or downward sloping when the
price is trending
when the price is range bound the moving
average will be flat and the price
fluctuates to both extremes
the key while trading mean reversions
using the moving averages is to
understand the trend and trade the
reversion accordingly
the trend is the force and the bigger
picture that one must always keep in
mind while trading mean reversions
one easy yet effective way to determine
the trend is using Dow Theory a topic
that we have covered in detail in one of
our earlier videos
for example in this weekly chart of the
S P 500 the index has been in an uptrend
for much longer periods than it has been
in a downtrend that's the characteristic
of all broad market indices as they
consistently shed losers and include new
winners that eventually take the index
higher
when the index was in an uptrend that is
when it was making higher highs and
higher lows the price seldom stayed
below the 10-week moving average and
spent a considerable time above the line
however it did revert several times to
the moving average which was the mean in
this case
as smart Trader could have traded the
situation by going long as price
approached the moving average whilst
exiting when prices move significantly
away
the opposite could have been profitable
during downtrends
let's now look at a widely used and
effective indicator for mean reversion
trading Bollinger Bands
this indicator is an adaptation of the
moving average it's made of a 20 period
simple moving average in the middle and
two standard deviations of the price of
the asset at both ends it gives a band
that makes it easy to identify and trade
Extremes in the price
the price hardly stays above and below
the extremes of the band and therefore
this range is a good starting point for
trades
the indicators can be more effective and
accurate when combined with another
indicator one of my favorites the macd
let's understand this with the same
example of the S P 500 index
the S P 500 chart is plotted with both
indicators as we can see when the price
was in an uptrend it stayed quite close
to the upper end of the Bollinger band
range
however the price reaching the upper
extreme was not a good enough signal by
itself to initiate a short trade the
price continued to stay elevated even
after the indicator flashed overbought
conditions
however when we look at this indicator
along with a macd it gives better Clues
on the strength of the trend and
therefore helps to identify the turning
points of the trend
here when the price was in the uptrend
there was hitting new highs and stayed
near the upper end of the Bollinger
range the macd was shouting weakness as
it stopped hitting newer highs
due to this weakness in the macd the
Bollinger band signal became much
clearer
in the same chart when the index was
bottoming out it was hitting new lows
and was staying near the lower end of
the Bollinger range
however the macd indicator was not
hitting new lows and bottomed before the
price
here too the Bollinger band signal of
price extreme when combined with macd
could have given a good reversal trade
another category of a mean reversion
trade is pairs trading in which Traders
take a pair of stocks correlated
positively or negatively and see if the
correlations have become Disturbed due
to the market extremes
for example if two companies are in the
same industry and one gets a higher
valuation multiple than the other the
trader could short the stock that
commands the higher valuation and buy
the stock that commands a lower
valuation betting that the valuations of
both the stocks will revert to mean
Traders also use algorithms to find pair
trades and to execute them in fact one
of the top hedge fund performers of the
last two decades The Medallion fund
traded these anomalies using mathematics
and algorithms and achieved very high
double-digit returns
trading pairs for mean reversion is
quite a complex exercise and isn't
advisable until you gain significant
expertise in the markets and know how
the correlations work between stocks and
industries
in mean reversion trading it's always
essential to double or even triple check
your entry points because you're betting
on the reversal of the trend and Trends
can often last for long periods which
lowers the probability of success
when you enter at the right point with a
low risk exit in place you can trade the
mean reversals much more confidently
regular viewers will know that I use a
form of mean reversion but only for
well-diversified funds whereas I use
breakout trading for trend continuations
for all of my singular stock positions
anyone interested in my strategy can of
course join our group providing access
to my PDF and our bespoke breakout
scanner both of which I use in the US
investing championships
irrespective of the strategy you choose
you must always have a risk management
plan in force the risk of Ruin is much
higher in mean reversion trading for
single stocks Therefore your first
priority must be to build a skill of
preserving your capital
everything else will gradually fall into
place
thanks for watching
